skill.name: devflow-tdd-enforcer
skill.description: Enforces test-first task ordering in TASKS.md by blocking implementation completion before related tests are complete.
skill.summary: Blocks TDD order violations in DevFlow task lists.
skill.author: Dimon94

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported shell execution, weak cryptography, and reconnaissance patterns, but review found these are Markdown examples, inline code, and configuration text in SKILL.md. No executable code, network behavior, credential access, data exfiltration, or prompt injection evidence was found. The skill is low risk because it describes a blocking guardrail that can affect workflow behavior, but it is safe to publish.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Markdown Shell Execution Markers
low_findings.description: Static analysis flagged backticks as Ruby or shell execution. These locations are Markdown inline code, fenced examples, regex text, and configuration snippets, not executable code. Verdict: FALSE_POSITIVE.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed file is Markdown documentation and the cited text appears inside inline code or fenced blocks. No runnable Ruby, shell, hook script, or command invocation exists in the package.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Weak Cryptographic Algorithm
low_findings.description: Static analysis flagged weak cryptography at lines containing ordinary prose or configuration description. No hashing, encryption, signature, or digest operation is present. Verdict: FALSE_POSITIVE.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines contain the skill description, a JSON description field, and a heading. I did not find evidence of cryptographic APIs or weak algorithm use.

low_findings.title: False Positive: System Reconnaissance
low_findings.description: Static analysis flagged reconnaissance terms in task examples and rationale text. The cited lines describe sample TDD tasks and documentation, not host discovery or data collection. Verdict: FALSE_POSITIVE.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited content is explanatory task-list text with no commands such as uname, whoami, id, or environment enumeration. There is no mechanism for reconnaissance in the skill package.

content.user_title: Enforce TDD Order in Task Lists
content.value_statement: Teams often mark implementation work complete before tests are written or verified. This skill defines a guardrail that blocks those task-list violations in DevFlow workflows.
actual_capabilities: Defines blocked and correct TDD ordering patterns for TASKS.md files.
Documents guardrail behavior for PreToolUse edits to DevFlow requirement tasks.
Provides regex patterns for detecting implementation-before-test task completion.
Explains user-facing block messages and remediation steps.
Documents skip options for session, file marker, and environment override workflows.
limitations: The package contains guidance only and no executable enforcement script.
Detection depends on task wording that includes test and implementation terms.
Skip conditions can bypass enforcement when users intentionally override it.
It focuses on task order and does not verify real test quality or coverage.

use_cases.title: Keep Feature Work Test First
use_cases.description: Block task-list updates that mark implementation complete before related test work is done.

use_cases.title: Standardize DevFlow Reviews
use_cases.description: Give reviewers a consistent rule for spotting TDD order violations in requirements task files.

use_cases.title: Document Guardrail Behavior
use_cases.description: Explain expected block messages, skip conditions, and task sequencing to teams adopting DevFlow.

prompt_templates.title: Check a Task List
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner review of one requirements task file.
prompt_templates.prompt: Review my TASKS.md file for TDD order issues. Identify any implementation tasks completed before related test tasks.

prompt_templates.title: Fix TDD Ordering
prompt_templates.prompt: Reorder these DevFlow tasks so each test task appears before its implementation task. Keep task IDs and user story labels intact.

prompt_templates.title: Draft a Block Message
prompt_templates.scenario: Create clear remediation text for a failed edit.
prompt_templates.prompt: Write a concise block message for a TASKS.md edit that violates TDD order. Include the failed rule and the next action.

prompt_templates.title: Audit Guardrail Coverage
prompt_templates.prompt: Evaluate whether these TDD guardrail patterns cover implementation-before-test, missing-test, and user-story ordering violations. Note gaps and false positives.

output_examples.input: A task list shows an implementation task marked complete while the next related test task is still open.
output_examples.output: The edit should be blocked. Move the test task before the implementation task, then complete the test task first.

output_examples.input: A hotfix task file includes a documented skip marker with a follow-up test task.
output_examples.output: The guardrail can be bypassed for this file, but the follow-up test task should remain visible and tracked.

output_examples.input: A user story has test and implementation tasks both complete in the correct order.
output_examples.output: No TDD order violation is present. The task sequence matches the documented guardrail rule.

best_practices: Write explicit test tasks before implementation tasks for each user story.
Use skip markers only with a clear reason and a tracked follow-up test.
Review TASKS.md changes before marking implementation work complete.
anti_patterns: Marking implementation tasks complete while related tests remain incomplete.
Using vague task names that hide whether work is test or implementation.
Adding broad skip overrides for routine feature work.

faq.question: Does this skill run tests?
faq.answer: No. It documents task-order enforcement and does not execute a test suite.

faq.question: Does it include executable hook code?
faq.answer: No. The reviewed package contains only SKILL.md documentation.

faq.question: What files does it target?
faq.answer: It targets DevFlow requirements task files under devflow/requirements paths.

faq.question: Can users bypass the guardrail?
faq.answer: Yes. The documentation describes session, file marker, and environment override skip conditions.

faq.question: Does it prove test coverage?
faq.answer: No. It enforces task order only and does not measure coverage or test quality.

faq.question: Which tools can use it?
faq.answer: The report lists compatibility with Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.
